On this page

Bound States, Resonances, and Scattering Theory ​

This article presents a comprehensive treatment of three interconnected topics in quantum mechanics: the existence conditions for bound states in various potentials, the nature of resonance states and their relationship to bound states, and the general framework of scattering theory. Detailed mathematical derivations are provided throughout.

Part I: Existence Conditions for Bound States ​

1.1 One-Dimensional Symmetric Finite Square Well ​

Consider a particle of mass μ moving in the potential:

V(x)={−V0,|x|<a0,|x|>a

where V0>0 represents the well depth. We seek bound state solutions with energy E<0.

Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation

The stationary Schrödinger equation is:

−ℏ22μd2ψdx2+V(x)ψ=Eψ

Inside the well region where |x|<a:

−ℏ22μd2ψdx2−V0ψ=Eψ

Rearranging:

d2ψdx2=−2μ(E+V0)ℏ2ψ

Since −V0<E<0 for bound states, we have E+V0>0. Defining:

k=2μ(E+V0)ℏ

The equation becomes:

d2ψdx2=−k2ψ

The general solution is:

ψ(x)=Acos⁡(kx)+Bsin⁡(kx)

Outside the well where |x|>a and V(x)=0:

d2ψdx2=−2μEℏ2ψ=2μ|E|ℏ2ψ

Defining:

κ=−2μEℏ=2μ|E|ℏ

The equation becomes:

d2ψdx2=κ2ψ

For normalizable bound states, the solution must decay exponentially:

ψ(x)={Ceκx,x<−aDe−κx,x>a

Key Constraint

Adding the squares of k and κ:

k2+κ2=2μ(E+V0)ℏ2+−2μEℏ2=2μV0ℏ2

This can be written as:

ξ2+η2=R2

where ξ=ka, η=κa, and R=2μV0a/ℏ is a dimensionless measure of the well strength.

Even Parity Solutions

For ψ(−x)=ψ(x), we have B=0 inside the well, and by symmetry C=D outside. The wavefunction is:

ψ(x)={Ceκx,x<−aAcos⁡(kx),−a<x<aCe−κx,x>a

Applying Boundary Conditions at x=a

Continuity of ψ:

Acos⁡(ka)=Ce−κa

Continuity of ψ′:

dψdx|x=a−=−Aksin⁡(ka)dψdx|x=a+=−Cκe−κa

Therefore:

−Aksin⁡(ka)=−Cκe−κa

Dividing the derivative continuity equation by the wavefunction continuity equation:

−Aksin⁡(ka)Acos⁡(ka)=−Cκe−κaCe−κa

Simplifying:

−ktan⁡(ka)=−κktan⁡(ka)=κ

Odd Parity Solutions

For ψ(−x)=−ψ(x), we have A=0 inside, and by antisymmetry C=−D outside (with the sign convention that ψ(x<−a)=−Ceκx gives continuity). The wavefunction is:

ψ(x)={−Ceκx,x<−aBsin⁡(kx),−a<x<aCe−κx,x>a

Applying Boundary Conditions at x=a

Continuity of ψ:

Bsin⁡(ka)=Ce−κa

Continuity of ψ′:

Bkcos⁡(ka)=−Cκe−κa

Dividing:

Bkcos⁡(ka)Bsin⁡(ka)=−Cκe−κaCe−κakcot⁡(ka)=−κ−kcot⁡(ka)=κ

Graphical Solution and Existence Conditions

Using dimensionless variables ξ=ka and η=κa:

For even parity: η=ξtan⁡ξ For odd parity: η=−ξcot⁡ξ

Both subject to: ξ2+η2=R2

Analysis of Even Parity Solutions

The function η=ξtan⁡ξ starts at η=0 when ξ=0 and increases monotonically until ξ=π/2 where it diverges to +∞. The circle ξ2+η2=R2 always intersects this curve at least once for any R>0 because:

  • At ξ=0, the curve has η=0
  • At small ξ, tan⁡ξ≈ξ, so η≈ξ2 which is below the circle
  • As ξ increases, the curve ξtan⁡ξ eventually rises above the circle

Therefore, at least one even parity bound state always exists for any attractive 1D potential well, no matter how shallow.

Analysis of Odd Parity Solutions

The function η=−ξcot⁡ξ starts at η=1 when ξ=0 (using limξ→0(−ξcot⁡ξ)=1) and decreases, crossing zero at ξ=π/2, then diverging to −∞ at ξ=π.

For an intersection to exist, the circle must extend beyond ξ=π/2, requiring:

R>π2

In physical terms:

2μV0aℏ>π2

Squaring:

2μV0a2ℏ2>π248μV0a2>π2ℏ2

This is the condition for at least one odd parity bound state to exist. The physical interpretation is that odd parity requires a node at the origin, which needs sufficient "space" in the well.

Figure 1: (Left) The finite square well potential with bound state energy levels. (Right) Even and odd parity wave functions showing the characteristic exponential decay outside the well and oscillatory behavior inside.

Figure 2: Wave function matching at the potential boundaries. The wave function and its derivative must be continuous at x=±a, leading to the transcendental equations for bound state energies.

1.2 One-Dimensional Semi-Infinite Square Well ​

Consider a potential with an infinite wall at x=0:

V(x)={∞,x<0−V0,0<x<a0,x>a

Boundary Condition at x=0

The infinite potential requires:

ψ(0)=0

Inside the well (0<x<a), the general solution is:

ψ(x)=Asin⁡(kx)+Bcos⁡(kx)

Applying ψ(0)=0:

B=0

So ψ(x)=Asin⁡(kx) automatically satisfies the boundary condition.

Outside (x>a):

ψ(x)=Ce−κx

Matching at x=a

Continuity of ψ:

Asin⁡(ka)=Ce−κa

Continuity of ψ′:

Akcos⁡(ka)=−Cκe−κa

Dividing:

kcot⁡(ka)=−κκ=−kcot⁡(ka)

This is identical to the odd parity condition of the symmetric well. The infinite wall enforces a node at the origin, equivalent to requiring odd parity symmetry.

Existence Condition

The condition for at least one bound state is:

8μV0a2>π2ℏ2

Unlike the symmetric well, there is no guaranteed bound state here—the infinite wall "wastes" half of the well for the ground state.

1.3 Delta Function Potential ​

For an attractive delta function potential V(x)=−γδ(x) with γ>0:

Schrödinger Equation

−ℏ22μd2ψdx2−γδ(x)ψ=Eψ

Boundary Condition at x=0

Integrate the Schrödinger equation from −ϵ to +ϵ:

−ℏ22μ∫−ϵ+ϵd2ψdx2dx−γ∫−ϵ+ϵδ(x)ψ(x)dx=E∫−ϵ+ϵψ(x)dx

Taking the limit ϵ→0:

  • First term: −ℏ22μ[ψ′(0+)−ψ′(0−)]
  • Second term: −γψ(0)
  • Third term: 0 (finite integrand over zero width)

Therefore:

ψ′(0+)−ψ′(0−)=−2μγℏ2ψ(0)

For an even parity bound state (the only possibility for a single delta function):

ψ(x)=ψ(−x)⇒ψ′(0+)=−ψ′(0−)

Substituting:

2ψ′(0+)=−2μγℏ2ψ(0)ψ′(0+)=−μγℏ2ψ(0)

Solution for x≠0

For x>0 where V(x)=0:

ψ(x)=Ae−κx

where κ=−2μE/ℏ.

At x=0+:

ψ(0+)=Aψ′(0+)=−Aκ

Applying the Boundary Condition

−Aκ=−μγℏ2Aκ=μγℏ2

Energy Eigenvalue

−2μE=μγℏ

Squaring both sides:

−2μE=μ2γ2ℏ2E=−μγ22ℏ2

Normalization

∫−∞+∞|ψ|2dx=2∫0∞A2e−2κxdx=2A2⋅12κ=A2κ=1

Therefore:

A=κ=μγℏ2

Complete Wavefunction

ψ(x)=μγℏ2exp⁡(−μγ|x|ℏ2)

Key insight: There is exactly one bound state, regardless of how weak the potential is. This is a characteristic feature of 1D attractive potentials.

1.4 Shallow Well Approximation ​

For a finite square well with small R=2μV0a/ℏ≪1:

Ground State Energy

For the even parity ground state, when R≪1, we expect ka≪1 and κa≪1.

Using tan⁡(ka)≈ka for small ka:

k⋅ka≈κk2a≈κ

From the constraint k2+κ2=R2/a2 and the fact that κ≪k when ka≪1:

k2≈R2a2k≈Ra

Therefore:

κ≈k2a=R2a

The energy is:

E=−ℏ2κ22μ=−ℏ2R42μa2=−2μV02a2ℏ2

Probability Inside the Well

The normalized wavefunction inside is approximately constant ψ(x)≈B since ka≪1.

Outside: ψ(x)=Ae−κ|x| where A≈B by continuity at x=a.

Normalization integral:

∫−∞∞|ψ|2dx=2∫0aB2dx+2∫a∞A2e−2κxdx=1

With A≈B:

2B2a+2B2e−2κa2κ=1

Since κa=R2≪1, e−2κa≈1:

2B2a+B2κ≈B2(2a+aR2)≈B2aR2=1

Therefore:

B2=R2a

Probability inside the well:

Pin=2B2a=2R2≪1

Remarkable Result: In a shallow well, the particle is mostly found outside the well, even though it is bound. This is consistent with the uncertainty principle—a shallow well corresponds to a weakly bound, spatially extended state.

1.5 Three-Dimensional Spherical Square Well ​

For a central potential:

V(r)={−V0,r<a0,r>a

Radial Schrödinger Equation

For a central potential, ψ(r)=Rl(r)Ylm(θ,ϕ). The radial equation is:

−ℏ22μ1r2ddr(r2dRldr)+[V(r)+ℏ2l(l+1)2μr2]Rl=ERl

Substituting Rl(r)=χl(r)/r:

−ℏ22μd2χldr2+[V(r)+ℏ2l(l+1)2μr2]χl=Eχl

Lowest Energy State (l=0)

For s-wave (l=0), the effective potential has no centrifugal barrier. The equation becomes:

−ℏ22μd2χdr2+V(r)χ=Eχ

With boundary condition χ(0)=0 (since R=χ/r must be finite at r=0).

Solution

Inside (r<a):

χ(r)=Asin⁡(kr)

Outside (r>a):

χ(r)=Be−κr

Matching at r=a

Continuity of χ:

Asin⁡(ka)=Be−κa

Continuity of χ′:

Akcos⁡(ka)=−Bκe−κa

Dividing:

kcot⁡(ka)=−κκ=−kcot⁡(ka)

This is identical to the 1D semi-infinite well!

Existence Condition

The condition for at least one bound state is:

R=2μV0aℏ>π2

or:

8μV0a2ℏ2>π2

Key Difference from 1D: In 3D, a sufficiently shallow well supports no bound states. This contrasts with 1D where any attractive potential binds at least one state. The physical reason is that in 3D, the boundary condition χ(0)=0 effectively creates a "centrifugal-like" constraint even for s-wave, making it easier for the particle to escape.

Part II: Resonance States and Quasi-Bound States ​

2.1 Physical Picture of Resonance ​

A resonance state occurs when a particle with energy E>0 has a high probability of being temporarily trapped in a potential region, despite having sufficient energy to escape to infinity.

Characteristics:

  • The particle remains in the potential region for a characteristic time τ
  • The energy is not sharply defined but has a width Γ
  • The energy-time uncertainty relation gives: τ≈ℏ/Γ

2.2 Double-Barrier Resonance Structure ​

Consider a potential well surrounded by finite barriers:

Figure 3: A double-barrier structure creates a quasi-bound state. The particle is temporarily trapped in the well region, with the wave function amplitude significantly enhanced inside at resonance energies.

Model Potential

V(x)={0,0<x<aV0,a<x<a+b(V0>E)0,x>a+b

with V(x)=∞ for x<0.

Wavefunctions in Each Region

For 0<E<V0:

  • Region I (0<x<a): ψI=Asin⁡(kx) where k=2μE/ℏ
  • Region II (a<x<a+b): ψII=Beαx+Ce−αx where α=2μ(V0−E)/ℏ
  • Region III (x>a+b): ψIII=Dsin⁡(kx+ϕ)

Matching Conditions at x=a

Continuity of ψ:

Asin⁡(ka)=Beαa+Ce−αa

Continuity of ψ′:

Akcos⁡(ka)=Bαeαa−Cαe−αa

Matching Conditions at x=a+b

Continuity of ψ:

Beα(a+b)+Ce−α(a+b)=Dsin⁡(k(a+b)+ϕ)

Continuity of ψ′:

Bαeα(a+b)−Cαe−α(a+b)=Dkcos⁡(k(a+b)+ϕ)

Thick Barrier Limit (αb≫1)

When the barrier is thick, the term eα(a+b) dominates. The resonance condition approximately becomes:

ka=nπ

At these energies, the wavefunction inside the well has maximum amplitude due to constructive interference of multiply reflected waves.

2.3 Breit-Wigner Resonance Formula ​

Near a resonance energy En, the transmission coefficient takes the universal form:

Figure 4: The Breit-Wigner resonance profile showing the characteristic Lorentzian lineshape. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) equals Γ.

Derivation

The transmission amplitude can be written using the complex energy pole structure:

t(E)=Γ/2(E−En)+iΓ/2

where En is the resonance energy and Γ is the width.

The transmission coefficient is:

T(E)=|t(E)|2=(Γ/2)2(E−En)2+(Γ/2)2T(E)=Γ2/4(E−En)2+Γ2/4

Properties:

  1. At E=En: T=1 (perfect transmission at resonance)
  2. At E=En±Γ/2: T=1/2 (half-maximum)
  3. Γ is the full width at half maximum (FWHM)
  4. The integral over energy: ∫−∞∞T(E)dE=πΓ/2

2.4 Resonance Above a Potential Barrier ​

Consider a particle with E>V0 incident on a barrier:

V(x)={∞,x<0V0,0<x<a0,x>a

Wave Numbers

Inside the barrier: k′=2μ(E−V0)/ℏ Outside: k=2μE/ℏ

Wavefunctions

  • Region I (0<x<a): ψI=Asin⁡(k′x)
  • Region II (x>a): ψII=sin⁡(kx+ϕ) (normalized to unit amplitude)

Matching at x=a

Continuity of ψ:

Asin⁡(k′a)=sin⁡(ka+ϕ)

Continuity of ψ′:

Ak′cos⁡(k′a)=kcos⁡(ka+ϕ)

Dividing:

k′cot⁡(k′a)=kcot⁡(ka+ϕ)

Probability Inside

The amplitude inside the well:

|A|2=1sin2⁡(k′a)+(k′/k)2cos2⁡(k′a)

When sin⁡(k′a)=0, i.e., k′a=nπ:

|A|2=(kk′)2=EE−V0

For E≈V0+, this ratio becomes very large, indicating resonance.

Resonance Condition

k′a=nπ

Resonance Width

Expanding near resonance where kn′a=nπ and E≈En:

Let k′a=nπ+ϵ with ϵ≪1:

sin⁡(k′a)=sin⁡(nπ+ϵ)=(−1)nϵ≈(−1)n(k′−kn′)a

Using k′2=2μ(E−V0)/ℏ2, we have 2k′dk′=2μdE/ℏ2, so:

k′−kn′=μ(E−En)ℏ2kn′

Therefore:

sin⁡(k′a)≈(−1)nμa(E−En)ℏ2kn′

The amplitude becomes:

|A|2=En/V0(En−V0)/V0+μ2a2ℏ4kn′2(E−En)2

Comparing with the Breit-Wigner form, the width is:

Γ=4ℏ(En−V0)a2μV0

2.5 Decaying States and Complex Energy ​

For a true decaying state, we use outgoing wave boundary conditions.

Wavefunction Ansatz

ψ(x)={Asin⁡(k′x),0<x<aeikx,x>a

Note: The outgoing wave eikx in region x>a represents a particle escaping to +∞.

Matching at x=a

Continuity of ψ:

Asin⁡(k′a)=eika

Continuity of ψ′:

Ak′cos⁡(k′a)=ikeika

Dividing:

k′cot⁡(k′a)=ik

Complex Solution

Since k=2μE/ℏ and k′=2μ(E−V0)/ℏ are related through energy, and the right side is imaginary while the left side is real for real E, we need complex energy.

Near resonance where k′a=nπ+ϵ:

cot⁡(k′a)≈−ϵ=−(k′−kn′)a

So:

−k′(k′−kn′)a=ik

For k≪k′ (low energy outside compared to inside):

k′−kn′=−ikk′a

The energy is:

E=V0+ℏ2k′22μ=V0+ℏ2kn′22μ−iℏ2knkμa

Writing E=En−iΓ/2:

Γ=2ℏ2knμa

Time Evolution

With complex energy:

ψ(t)∼e−iEt/ℏ=e−iEnt/ℏe−Γt/(2ℏ)

Probability decays exponentially:

|ψ(t)|2∼e−Γt/ℏ=e−t/τ

where the lifetime is:

τ=ℏΓ

This is consistent with the energy-time uncertainty principle.

2.6 Connection Between Resonance and Bound States ​

As the barrier height V0→∞:

  • The resonance energy Er approaches the bound state energy Eb
  • The width Γ→0
  • The lifetime τ→∞

Unified Picture:

  • Bound state: Infinite barrier height (particle permanently trapped)
  • Resonance: Finite barrier (particle temporarily trapped)
  • Scattering state: No barrier or energy far from resonance

Part III: Scattering Theory Fundamentals ​

3.1 Basic Setup and Asymptotic Behavior ​

Figure 6: Scattering geometry showing the incident plane wave and the scattered spherical wave. The scattering amplitude f(θ) characterizes the amplitude for scattering in direction θ.

Consider a particle incident on a localized potential V(r) that vanishes as r→∞. The time-independent Schrödinger equation is:

(−ℏ22μ∇2+V(r))ψ(r)=Eψ(r)

For scattering states, E>0. We define k=2μE/ℏ.

Asymptotic Form of the Wavefunction

At large distances from the scattering center (r→∞), the wavefunction consists of two parts:

  1. The incident plane wave: eikz (propagating along the z-direction)
  2. The scattered spherical wave: f(θ,ϕ)eikrr
ψ(r)→r→∞eikz+f(θ,ϕ)eikrr

The scattering amplitude f(θ,ϕ) contains all information about the scattering process.

Physical Interpretation:

  • The factor eikr/r represents an outgoing spherical wave (conservation of probability flux requires the 1/r dependence)
  • The scattering amplitude f(θ,ϕ) gives the amplitude for scattering in direction (θ,ϕ)

3.2 Cross Sections ​

Probability Current Density

The quantum mechanical probability current is:

j=ℏ2μi(ψ∗∇ψ−ψ∇ψ∗)

For the incident plane wave ψinc=eikz:

jinc=ℏkμz^=vz^

where v=ℏk/μ is the particle velocity.

For the scattered wave ψsc=f(θ,ϕ)eikrr:

jsc⋅r^=ℏkμ|f(θ,ϕ)|2r2=v|f(θ,ϕ)|2r2

Differential Cross Section

The differential cross section is defined as the ratio of scattered flux per unit solid angle to incident flux:

dσdΩ=r2jsc⋅r^|jinc|=|f(θ,ϕ)|2dσdΩ=|f(θ,ϕ)|2

Total Cross Section

σtot=∫|f(θ,ϕ)|2dΩ=∫02πdϕ∫0πdθsin⁡θ|f(θ,ϕ)|2

3.3 Optical Theorem ​

Derivation

From probability conservation (continuity equation), the total flux into a large sphere must equal the total flux out. The interference between incident and scattered waves in the forward direction leads to a relationship between the total cross section and the forward scattering amplitude.

Consider a large sphere of radius R. The net flux through the surface is:

∮j⋅dA=0

Breaking this into incident, scattered, and interference terms, and using the fact that the incident flux through a closed surface is zero, we obtain:

σtot=4πkIm[f(0)]σtot=4πkImf(0)

where f(0) is the scattering amplitude in the forward direction (θ=0).

Physical Interpretation: The total cross section (which includes scattering in all directions) is proportional to the imaginary part of the forward scattering amplitude. This arises because the scattered wave in the forward direction interferes with the incident wave, depleting the beam.

3.4 Partial Wave Expansion ​

For central potentials V(r), the scattering amplitude depends only on θ (not ϕ), and we can expand in Legendre polynomials.

Expansion of Plane Wave

A plane wave can be expanded in spherical waves:

eikz=∑l=0∞(2l+1)iljl(kr)Pl(cos⁡θ)

where jl(kr) are spherical Bessel functions and Pl(cos⁡θ) are Legendre polynomials.

Asymptotic Behavior of Spherical Bessel Functions

As r→∞:

jl(kr)→r→∞sin⁡(kr−lπ/2)kr=12ikr[ei(kr−lπ/2)−e−i(kr−lπ/2)]

So the plane wave becomes:

eikz→r→∞∑l=0∞(2l+1)il2ikrPl(cos⁡θ)[ei(kr−lπ/2)−e−i(kr−lπ/2)]

Partial Wave Expansion of Scattered Wave

For each partial wave (angular momentum l), the effect of the potential is to introduce a phase shift δl. The asymptotic form of the radial wave function for each l is:

ul(r)r→r→∞1krsin⁡(kr−lπ/2+δl)

Comparing with the free particle case (δl=0), we see that δl represents the phase shift introduced by the potential.

Scattering Amplitude

The total wavefunction is:

ψ(r)=∑l=0∞ul(r)rPl(cos⁡θ)

Matching the asymptotic form:

ψ(r)→r→∞eikz+f(θ)eikrr

we obtain:

f(θ)=∑l=0∞(2l+1)e2iδl−12ikPl(cos⁡θ)

Defining the partial wave amplitude:

fl=e2iδl−12ik=1kcot⁡δl−ik=eiδlsin⁡δlk

The full scattering amplitude is:

f(θ)=∑l=0∞(2l+1)flPl(cos⁡θ)=1k∑l=0∞(2l+1)eiδlsin⁡δlPl(cos⁡θ)

3.5 Partial Wave Cross Sections ​

Total Cross Section

Using the orthogonality of Legendre polynomials:

∫−11Pl(x)Pl′(x)dx=22l+1δll′

The total cross section becomes:

σtot=∫|f(θ)|2dΩ=2π∫−11|f(θ)|2d(cos⁡θ)=2π∑l=0∞(2l+1)2|fl|2⋅22l+1σtot=4πk2∑l=0∞(2l+1)sin2⁡δl

Partial Wave Cross Section

For each angular momentum l:

σl=4πk2(2l+1)sin2⁡δl

Maximum Value (Unitarity Bound)

Since sin2⁡δl≤1:

σlmax=4πk2(2l+1)

This maximum is achieved when δl=π/2 (modulo π).

3.6 Low-Energy Scattering: s-Wave Dominance ​

At low energies where ka≪1 (where a is the range of the potential), higher partial waves (l≥1) are suppressed.

Effective Potential Analysis

The radial equation includes an effective centrifugal barrier:

Veff(r)=V(r)+ℏ2l(l+1)2μr2

For l≥1, the centrifugal term ∼ℏ2l(l+1)/(2μr2) prevents the particle from reaching small r. At low energies, the particle does not have enough energy to overcome this barrier, so scattering is dominated by s-wave (l=0).

Condition for s-Wave Dominance

The centrifugal barrier is significant when:

ℏ2l(l+1)2μa2≳E=ℏ2k22μ

For l=1:

ka≲2

In practice, for ka≪1, only s-wave contributes significantly.

3.7 Scattering Length and Effective Range ​

Scattering Length Definition

For s-wave scattering at low energies, we define the scattering length as by the expansion:

kcot⁡δ0=−1as+12rek2+O(k4)

where re is the effective range.

At very low energies (k→0):

kcot⁡δ0→k→0−1as

Low-Energy Scattering Amplitude

f0=1kcot⁡δ0−ik=1−1/as−ik=−as1+ikas

For kas≪1:

f0≈−as

Low-Energy Cross Section

σ0=4π|f0|2≈4πas2

This is isotropic (independent of angle) because only s-wave contributes.

3.8 Connection Between Scattering Length and Bound States ​

Weakly Bound State

If the potential supports a weakly bound s-state with binding energy Eb=−ℏ2β2/(2μ) where β≪1/a, the scattering length is:

as=1β=ℏ−2μEb

Derivation

For a bound state at E=−ℏ2β2/(2μ)<0, the radial wavefunction outside the potential is u(r)∝e−βr.

The logarithmic derivative at the boundary is:

u′u|r=a=−β

Matching to the scattering solution, this corresponds to a pole in the scattering amplitude at imaginary k=iβ. The condition for a pole in the s-wave amplitude:

kcot⁡δ0=ik

At k=iβ:

iβcot⁡δ0(iβ)=i(iβ)=−βcot⁡δ0(iβ)=−1

Using the effective range expansion at low energy:

kcot⁡δ0≈−1as

For a weakly bound state where the bound state pole is close to threshold:

−1as≈−βas≈1β

Physical Interpretation:

The scattering length measures the "size" of the bound state. For a loosely bound state (small binding energy ⇒ small β), the scattering length is large, leading to a large low-energy cross-section. This is why low-energy nucleon-nucleon scattering has a large cross-section—the deuteron is a very loosely bound state with binding energy only 2.2 MeV.

Sign of Scattering Length:

  • as>0: Potential is attractive enough to support a bound state (or nearly so)
  • as<0: Potential is attractive but not strong enough for a bound state
  • as→±∞: Zero-energy resonance (bound state exactly at threshold)

3.9 Born Approximation ​

First Born Approximation

For weak potentials or high energies, we can treat the scattering perturbatively. The first Born approximation gives:

fB(k′,k)=−μ2πℏ2∫e−iq⋅r′V(r′)d3r′

where q=k′−k is the momentum transfer.

For a spherically symmetric potential:

fB(θ)=−2μℏ2q∫0∞rV(r)sin⁡(qr)dr

where q=2ksin⁡(θ/2).

Validity Condition

The Born approximation is valid when the scattered wave is much smaller than the incident wave. This requires:

μℏ2k∫0∞|V(r)|dr≪1

or equivalently:

ka≫μ|V|a2ℏ2

For a potential of strength V0 and range a, this is roughly:

ka≫μV0a2ℏ2

Comparison with Partial Wave Method:

  • Born approximation: Good for high energies (ka≫1) and weak potentials
  • Partial wave method: Good for low energies (ka≪1) where only a few partial waves contribute

3.10 Levinson's Theorem ​

Figure 5: Illustration of Levinson's theorem showing the difference in zero-energy phase shifts for potentials with and without bound states. Each bound state contributes π to δ0(0).

Theorem Statement

The phase shift at zero energy is related to the number of bound states by:

δl(0)=nlπ

where nl is the number of bound states with angular momentum l.

Proof Outline

Consider the Jost function fl(k), which is analytic in the upper half of the complex k-plane. The Jost function is defined such that the regular solution of the radial Schrödinger equation behaves as:

ul(r)→r→∞12[fl(k)ei(kr−lπ/2)−fl∗(k)e−i(kr−lπ/2)]

Bound states correspond to zeros of fl(k) on the positive imaginary axis at kn=iκn (where En=−ℏ2κn2/2μ<0).

The phase shift is related to the argument of the Jost function:

δl(k)=−arg⁡fl(k)

As k varies from 0 to ∞ along the real axis, the change in the argument of fl(k) is related to the number of zeros in the upper half-plane by the argument principle.

Since fl(k)→1 as k→∞ (no scattering at infinite energy), we have δl(∞)=0.

The number of bound states equals the change in phase divided by π:

nl=1π[δl(0)−δl(∞)]=δl(0)π

Therefore:

δl(0)=nlπ

Physical Significance:

  • Each bound state contributes π to the zero-energy phase shift
  • The phase shift encodes information about the discrete spectrum
  • For attractive potentials that support bound states, δ0(0)>0

Example:

  • Square well with no bound state: δ0(0)=0
  • Square well with one bound state: δ0(0)=π

3.11 Resonant Scattering ​

Phase Shift Near Resonance

When there is a resonance at energy Er with width Γ, the phase shift behaves as:

tan⁡δl(E)=Γ/2Er−E

or equivalently:

δl(E)=tan−1⁡(Γ/2Er−E)

Behavior at Resonance:

  • At E≪Er: δl≈0
  • At E=Er: δl=π/2
  • At E≫Er: δl≈π

The phase shift increases rapidly by π as energy passes through the resonance.

Cross Section at Resonance

At E=Er where δl=π/2:

σl(Er)=4πk2(2l+1)sin2⁡(π/2)=4πk2(2l+1)

This is the unitarity limit—the maximum possible cross section for that partial wave.

Physical Interpretation:

At resonance, the particle is temporarily trapped in a quasi-bound state, leading to a large scattering probability. The rapid change in phase shift by π is the signature of resonance.

3.12 Unitarity Bound and General Properties ​

Optical Theorem Revisited

From the partial wave expansion:

f(0)=1k∑l=0∞(2l+1)eiδlsin⁡δl

Taking the imaginary part:

Imf(0)=1k∑l=0∞(2l+1)sin2⁡δl

Comparing with the total cross section:

σtot=4πk2∑l=0∞(2l+1)sin2⁡δl=4πkImf(0)

This confirms the optical theorem derived earlier.

Maximum Cross Section

For any given partial wave, the maximum contribution to the cross section is achieved when sin2⁡δl=1, i.e., δl=π/2.

σlmax=4πk2(2l+1)

This is called the unitarity bound because it follows from the unitarity of the S-matrix (conservation of probability).

S-Matrix Formulation

The S-matrix for each partial wave is:

Sl=e2iδl

Unitarity requires |Sl|=1, which is automatically satisfied by this form.

The relationship to the scattering amplitude:

fl=Sl−12ik

The total cross section can be written as:

σtot=πk2∑l=0∞(2l+1)|Sl−1|2

Summary and Key Formulas ​

Bound State Existence ​

DimensionCondition
1D (symmetric well)At least one bound state always exists
1D (semi-infinite)Requires 8μV0a2>π2ℏ2
3D (spherical well)Requires 8μV0a2>π2ℏ2

Resonance Phenomena ​

  • Breit-Wigner formula: T(E)=Γ2/4(E−Er)2+Γ2/4
  • Lifetime-width relation: τ=ℏ/Γ
  • Phase shift near resonance: tan⁡δ=Γ/2Er−E

Scattering Theory ​

Basic Definitions:

  • Scattering amplitude: f(θ,ϕ) defined by ψ→r→∞eikz+f(θ,ϕ)eikrr
  • Differential cross section: dσdΩ=|f(θ,ϕ)|2
  • Total cross section: σtot=∫|f|2dΩ
  • Optical theorem: σtot=4πkImf(0)

Partial Wave Analysis:

  • Partial wave amplitude: fl=e2iδl−12ik=eiδlsin⁡δlk
  • Scattering amplitude: f(θ)=∑l=0∞(2l+1)flPl(cos⁡θ)
  • Total cross section: σtot=4πk2∑l=0∞(2l+1)sin2⁡δl
  • Unitarity bound: σlmax=4π(2l+1)k2

Low-Energy Scattering:

  • Scattering length: kcot⁡δ0→k→0−1as
  • Low-energy amplitude: f0≈−as
  • Low-energy cross section: σ0≈4πas2
  • Connection to bound states: as=ℏ−2μEb (for weakly bound states)

Levinson's Theorem:

  • δl(0)=nlπ where nl is the number of bound states

Born Approximation:

  • fB(k′,k)=−μ2πℏ2∫e−iq⋅r′V(r′)d3r′
  • Valid for weak potentials and high energies

Unified Picture ​

Bound states, resonances, and scattering states are unified through the analytic properties of the scattering amplitude:

  • Bound states: Poles on the positive imaginary k-axis (k=iβ, β>0)
  • Resonances: Poles near the real axis in the complex k-plane
  • Scattering states: Behavior along the real k-axis

This mathematical unity reflects the deep physical connection between these quantum mechanical phenomena.
